1:
ἀπόστολος — "apostle" — taken in its strictest sense here; Paul is insisting on equality with the Twelve, not merely on a lesser missionary function. οὐκ ἀπ᾽ ἀνθρώπων οὐδὲ δι᾽ ἀνθρώπου (ouk ap' anthrōpōn oude di' anthrōpou) — the double negative covers both the origin (apo, "from" = source) and the channel (dia, "through" = intermediary): Paul's apostolate derives from no human community and was transmitted through no human intermediary. The opening sentence is the most polemically loaded salutation in the NT — the argument of the entire letter is compressed into the first clause.

ἀλλὰ διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ καὶ θεοῦ πατρός — "but through Jesus Christ and God the Father" — the coordinate pairing of Christ and the Father as the joint source of apostolic commission is an implicit Christological claim: Christ stands on the divine side of the origin of Paul's call.
2: ταῖς ἐκκλησίαις τῆς Γαλατίας (tais ekklēsiais tēs Galatias) — "to the churches of Galatia." Ekklēsia (assembly, church) is the Greek civic term for the assembly of citizens called together for public deliberation, applied to the gathered community of believers. The plural "churches" indicates multiple congregations across the Galatian region, all receiving the same letter — an unusual circular format suggesting the issue being addressed was not local but regional, a coordinated effort by the agitators across multiple communities.
3: χάρις ὑμῖν καὶ εἰρήνη — "grace to you and peace" — the standard Pauline greeting fuses the Greek epistolary opener (chairein = greetings, reshaped to charis = grace) with the Hebrew/Aramaic shalom (peace). The pairing is not merely formulaic: grace and peace name the content of the gospel — unmerited favor from God and its fruit in restored relationship. ἀπὸ θεοῦ πατρὸς ἡμῶν καὶ κυρίου Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ — both God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ are presented as the joint origin of these gifts; the preposition apo (from) governs both, placing Christ coordinate with the Father as the source of saving grace.
4:
τοῦ δόντος ἑαυτὸν ὑπὲρ τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν ἡμῶν (tou dontos heauton hyper tōn hamartiōn hēmōn) — "who gave himself for our sins." The aorist participle dontos (having given) points to the single completed event of the cross; heauton (himself) emphasizes voluntariness — not something done to him but something he surrendered. The giving has a parallel in Titus 2:14 and the ransom-saying of Mark 10:45.

ὅπως ἐξέληται ἡμᾶς ἐκ τοῦ αἰῶνος τοῦ ἐνεστῶτος πονηροῦ — "to rescue us from the present evil age." Exaireō (to take out, rescue) has OT exodus resonance — God "taking out" Israel from Egypt. Aiōn ho enestōs ponēros (the present evil age) is the Jewish two-age schema: this age of sin and death is being displaced by the age to come, inaugurated in Christ's death and resurrection.
5: ᾧ ἡ δόξα εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων — "to whom be the glory forever and ever." The doxology at the start of the letter rather than the end is unusual; Paul typically reserves doxologies for conclusions (cf. Romans 11:36; Galatians 16:27). Its placement here grounds the entire theological argument that follows in an act of worship: the rescue from the present evil age (v4) is itself the occasion for praise. The definite article hē doxa (the glory) — not merely glory in general but the pre-eminent divine glory proper to God alone.
6:
μετατίθεσθε (metatithesthe) — "you are deserting." The word is regularly used in Greek for a deserter in war, a political turncoat, or a religious apostate — the most charged available term for switching allegiance. The present tense is significant: the defection is in process, not complete. Paul writes to arrest a movement still underway. Thaumazō (I am astonished) expresses genuine shock, not merely rhetorical emphasis — the speed of the desertion is what stuns Paul: houtōs tacheōs (so quickly).

ἀπὸ τοῦ καλέσαντος ὑμᾶς ἐν χάριτι Χριστοῦ — "from the one who called you by the grace of Christ." The object of desertion is not a doctrine but a person — the God who called them. The Galatians' adoption of the other gospel is not merely theological error but a relational abandonment.
7:
ὃ οὐκ ἔστιν ἄλλο — "which is not another gospel" — more precisely, "which is not of a different kind." The Greek allo (another of the same kind) vs. heteron (another of a different kind) distinction is technically relevant: Paul has said heteron euangelion in v6 (another-different gospel); here he says it is not allo (not another-same gospel) — it is no gospel at all.

οἱ ταράσσοντες ὑμᾶς καὶ θέλοντες μεταστρέψαι τὸ εὐαγγέλιον τοῦ Χριστοῦ — tarassō (to stir up, agitate, disturb) — used for the agitation of water and civic disturbances. Metastrephō (to turn completely around, pervert) — stronger than "change": a radical inversion of the gospel's character.
8:
ἀνάθεμα ἔστω (anathema estō) — "let him be accursed." Anathema (Greek rendering of Hebrew cherem) is the most extreme form of religious exclusion: devoted to destruction, cut off from the covenant community and placed under divine curse. The twice-stated anathema — in vv8 and 9 — frames the warning with the legal weight of double witness (Deuteronomy 19:15). The hypothetical extreme — "even if we ourselves or an angel from heaven" — is not rhetorical flourish but the establishment of an absolute: no source of authority whatsoever can legitimate a different gospel.

The irony is sharp: the agent who preaches another gospel falls under the same curse-structure that the genuine gospel announces as Christ's fate on behalf of sinners (Galatians 3:13: "cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree").
9: ὡς προειρήκαμεν (hōs proeirēkamen) — "as we said before." The "before" probably refers to Paul's previous visit — already at that stage the threat was visible and he had warned against it. The repetition now in writing doubles the witness: two formal statements, satisfying the Jewish-legal requirement of two witnesses to establish a matter (Deuteronomy 19:15). The anathema on the other-gospel-preacher is not personal anger but a legal verdict rendered twice for its binding force.
10: ἀνθρώπους πείθω ἢ τὸν θεόν (anthrōpous peithō ē ton theon) — "am I now seeking to please people, or God?" Peithō (to persuade, win approval, conciliate) targets the charge that Paul's law-free gospel was a people-pleasing accommodation to Gentile sensibilities — a softening of full Jewish covenant requirements to attract converts. The rhetorical question is anticipatory: the autobiography of vv11-24 will prove that his gospel came at enormous personal cost, not as a strategy for winning human favor. εἰ ἔτι ἀνθρώποις ἤρεσκον, Χριστοῦ δοῦλος οὐκ ἂν ἤμην — "if I were still trying to please people, I would not be a servant of Christ" — the present-unreal conditional establishes that the two postures are mutually exclusive.
11: τὸ εὐαγγέλιον τὸ εὐαγγελισθὲν ὑπ᾽ ἐμοῦ ὅτι οὐκ ἔστιν κατὰ ἄνθρωπον (kata anthrōpon) — "not of human origin" — literally "not according to the human standard or norm." Paul denies that his gospel fits the pattern of humanly-derived teaching, philosophical tradition, or institutional transmission. The passive euangelisthen (was proclaimed) hints at divine agency: the gospel was proclaimed through Paul but did not originate with him.
12: ἀλλὰ δι᾽ ἀποκαλύψεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (apokalypseōs Iēsou Christou) — "through a revelation of Jesus Christ." The genitive is likely both objective and subjective simultaneously: a revelation about Jesus Christ (its content) and a revelation from / given by Jesus Christ (its source). The Damascus encounter was simultaneously a disclosure of who Christ is and an act of divine self-disclosure by Christ himself. Apokalypsis (revelation, uncovering) contrasts with gradual human discovery: this was a sudden, unconditional divine disclosure that overturned Paul's entire prior framework.
13: τὴν ἐμὴν ἀναστροφήν ποτε ἐν τῷ Ἰουδαϊσμῷ (anastrophē) — "my former way of life in Judaism." Anastrophē (conduct, way of life, pattern of behavior) covers the entire complex of values, practices, and allegiances that constituted Paul's pre-conversion identity — not merely religious practice but who he was. Ioudaismos (Judaism) — a term Paul uses only here and v14 in the NT; it appears in 2 Maccabees for the distinctively Jewish way of life in opposition to Hellenistic assimilation. Paul does not disparage Judaism but describes his life as conducted within and for its advance.
14:
προέκοπτον ἐν τῷ Ἰουδαϊσμῷ ὑπὲρ πολλοὺς συνηλικιώτας (proekopton) — "I was advancing in Judaism beyond many of my own age." Prokoptō (to advance, cut a path forward, make progress) is an ambitious term implying swift movement toward a defined goal: Paul was not a passive participant but a high-achiever pressing past his contemporaries.

ζηλωτὴς ὑπάρχων τῶν πατρικῶν μου παραδόσεων — "extremely zealous for the traditions of my fathers." Zēlōtēs (zealot, one burning with zeal) connects Paul to the Phinehas-tradition (Numbers 25:11-13) of violent zeal for Torah-purity — the same zeal that drove his persecution of the church. The traditions of the fathers (patrikōn paradoseon) are the oral Torah, the interpretive framework built around the written law.
15: ὁ ἀφορίσας με ἐκ κοιλίας μητρός μου (ho aphorisas me ek koilias mētros mou) — "the one who set me apart from my mother's womb." Aphorizō (to set apart, separate, mark off) — the same root as Pharisaios (Pharisee = the separated one) and Paul's self-description in Romans 1:1 (aphorismenon eis euangelion = set apart for the gospel). The irony is profound: the Pharisee defined by separation from the impure discovers that God's prior act of separation was for the opposite purpose — to bring the gospel to the Gentiles, the very group from whom Pharisees separated. The prophetic echoes are deliberate: Jeremiah 1:5 ("before you were born I set you apart") and the Servant of Isaiah 49:1 ("before I was born the LORD called me") both shape Paul's self-understanding of a prenatal divine commission.
16:
ἀποκαλύψαι τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ ἐν ἐμοί (en emoi) — "to reveal his Son in me." The phrase is remarkable: not moi (to me, external vision) but en emoi (in me, interior transformation). The revelation was not merely visual data from outside but a life-reorienting disclosure within Paul's own being. This interior Christ is the theological ground of Paul's gospel: what he proclaims he received not as information passed on from others but as a living reality disclosed within him.

ἵνα εὐαγγελίζωμαι αὐτὸν ἐν τοῖς ἔθνεσιν — the purpose clause connects the interior revelation to the exterior mission: the in-me disclosure of the Son was given for the sake of the out-to-the-nations proclamation. The two en phrases (in me / among the Gentiles) frame the missionary paradox.
17: οὐδὲ ἀνῆλθον εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα πρὸς τοὺς πρὸ ἐμοῦ ἀποστόλους — "I did not go up to Jerusalem to those who were apostles before me." The deliberate non-consultation is Paul's primary biographical evidence: he had the opportunity to receive the gospel from the Jerusalem apostles and declined it. Going "up to Jerusalem" (always the verb for travel to that elevated city) was the natural move for any new convert seeking authorization; Paul instead went to Arabia and Damascus. The independence is geographic as well as theological.
18: ἀνῆλθον εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα ἱστορῆσαι Κηφᾶν (historesai Kēphan) — "I went up to Jerusalem to visit Cephas." Historeō (to inquire of, visit for information, get acquainted with) — the term implies purposeful personal acquaintance rather than official consultation. Paul went to get to know Peter (Cephas), not to receive instruction from him or obtain apostolic authorization. The fifteen-day stay was social and informational; the brevity itself argues against an apprenticeship reading.
19: ἕτερον δὲ τῶν ἀποστόλων οὐκ εἶδον, εἰ μὴ Ἰάκωβον τὸν ἀδελφὸν τοῦ κυρίου — "I saw none of the other apostles — only James the Lord's brother." The identification ton adelphon tou kyriou (the Lord's brother) uses the standard Greek term adelphos (sibling), the same word used throughout the NT for Jesus's brothers (Mark 3:31; Galatians 6:3; John 7:3). Whether this means biological brother, half-brother, or cousin has been debated since the patristic period; the phrase leaves the Greek ambiguous but the most natural reading of adelphos without qualification is a full brother.
20: ἰδοὺ ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ ὅτι οὐ ψεύδομαι — "before God I assure you that what I am writing is no lie." The divine witness invocation — "before God" — is the functional equivalent of a formal oath. Its presence here signals the severity of the accusations being answered: Paul's opponents were claiming his gospel was dependent on Jerusalem and was therefore a deviant simplification when stripped of circumcision and food laws. The solemn attestation establishes his autobiographical account as legally binding testimony, not rhetorical self-defense.
21: εἰς τὰ κλίματα τῆς Συρίας καὶ τῆς Κιλικίας (klimata) — "the regions of Syria and Cilicia." Klimata (zones, sectors, geographical areas) suggests a broad regional itinerary rather than specific city visits. Cilicia includes Tarsus, Paul's hometown; Syria includes Antioch, which became his primary missionary base. The spatial independence reinforces the theological argument: Paul operated in regions geographically distant from Jerusalem's immediate sphere.
22: ἤμην δὲ ἀγνοούμενος τῷ προσώπῳ ταῖς ἐκκλησίαις τῆς Ἰουδαίας — "I was personally unknown to the churches of Judea." Tō prosōpō (by face, in person) specifies that the anonymity was physical: the Judean churches did not know his face. The imperfect ēmyn agnoumenos (I continued unknown) implies ongoing anonymity across the period. This seals the independence argument: not only did Paul not receive his gospel from the Jerusalem apostles, the very communities that would have transmitted Jerusalem tradition did not know him by sight.
23: ὁ διώκων ἡμᾶς ποτε νῦν εὐαγγελίζεται τὴν πίστιν ἥν ποτε ἐπόρθει — "the one who formerly persecuted us is now proclaiming the faith he once tried to destroy." The verbal chiasm — diōkōn (persecuting) and eporthei (destroying) reversed by euangelizetai (now proclaiming) — structures the conversion as a radical inversion. Portheō (to devastate, ravage, destroy) is Paul's own word for his pre-conversion activity (v13); the Judean churches now use it in their report about him. The reversal is so complete that it becomes itself the occasion for praise.
24: καὶ ἐδόξαζον ἐν ἐμοὶ τὸν θεόν (edoxazon en emoi ton theon) — "and they glorified God because of me." The imperfect edoxazon (they kept on glorifying) describes ongoing, continuous praise. En emoi (in me, because of me) — the same construction as Galatians 1:16 ("to reveal his Son in me"): the same interior-in-me that was the location of God's revealing act is now the occasion of God's glorification among the Judean believers. The transformed persecutor is not merely a biographical curiosity; his existence as a living gospel-proclaimer is an ongoing act of worship rising from the Jewish church.
